retrieved_docs.title: Atopy -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The list of occupational allergens causing IgE-mediated allergic asthma includes animal products (obtained from cows, pigs, mice, dogs, cats, and horses), insect dust (from mealworms, storage mites, cockroaches, bees, and flies), plant products (such as fruits, vegetables, seeds, leaves, pollens, flours, plant, grain, and cotton dust, castor beans, tobacco, and weeping fig). Other allergens include gums and extracts (such as western red, California redwood, and exotic woods), microbial agents (fungal allergens, alginates, protozoa, bacteria, and fungi), enzymes (such as papain, hog trypsin, pancreatic extracts, subtilisin, and pineapple bromelain), therapeutic agents (such as penicillins, tetracycline, cephalosporins, sulfonamides, and spiramycin), sterilizing agents (such as chloramides), inorganic chemicals (such as metal fumes and salts, aluminum, cobalt, fluoride, nickel, platinum, vanadium, and zinc) and organic chemicals (such as amines, anhydrides, and azodicarbonamide). [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Wheat Allergy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Wheat allergy is the manifestation of mediator release, such as histamine, platelet activator factor, and leukotrienes, from mast cells and basophils. [1] [9] [10] The IgE production is thought to be due to a breach of oral tolerance, and as a result, of type 2 helper T cell-biased immune dysregulation that causes sensitization and B-cell IgE production. [11] [12] [13] [14] [15] Allergens found in wheat flour are alpha-purothionin, alpha-amylase/trypsin inhibitor, peroxidase, thioredoxin, lipid-protein transfer, serine proteinase inhibitor, thaumatin-like protein (TLP), gliadin, thiol reductase, 1-cys-peroxiredoxin, and serine protease-like inhibitor. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Clinical Significance -- Asthma
retrieved_docs.content: Allergen immunotherapy can reduce short-term symptoms in allergic asthma; however, there is a moderate increase in the risk of systemic and local reactions based on a meta-analysis. [6] A 3-year course of either sublingual or subcutaneous immunotherapy prevents asthma for up to 2 years in children and adolescents with grass/birch pollen that triggers moderate to severe allergic rhinitis. However, this still requires further research. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Food Allergies -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Cooking denatures the proteins responsible for cross-reactivity with pollen allergens, rendering them unrecognizable to the immune system. As a result, most individuals with oral allergy syndrome can tolerate cooked forms of these foods. Apples, peaches, cherries, and bananas are among the most common foods associated with oral allergy syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Allergy Immunotherapy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Allergenic proteins from pollen, dander, dust mites, insects, mold, among others are the main ingredient of allergen extract. However, the final product is a mixture of diluents or solvents and preservatives. Different extracts including aqueous, glycerinated, lyophilized, acetone precipitated, alum-precipitated are available. Diluents will keep the allergen in liquid form; commonly used agents are glycerin, phenol saline, and HSA. The staff should use measures that include good personal hygiene, hand washing, and antiseptics to clean working areas. These include a water-based disinfectant followed by the application of alcohol on working surfaces for preparing allergen extracts. Alcohol kills organisms by dehydration. Sanitization will prevent bacterial contamination. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Butterfly, Moth, and Caterpillar Envenomation -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Other notable venomous caterpillars in the US include the flannel moth ( Megalopyge crispata ), the lo moth ( Automeris io ), the saddleback moth ( Acharia stimulat a), and the hickory tiger moth ( Lophocampa caryae ). [2] [13] Lonomia obliqua caterpillars in South America, especially in Brazil, are infamous for causing severe pain and hemorrhagic syndrome. [14] [15] In Australia, caterpillars such as the mistletoe brown-tail moth ( Euproctis edwardsi ), processionary caterpillars ( Ochrogaster lunifer ), cup moths ( Doratifera spp. ), and the white-stemmed gum moth ( Chelepteryx colles i) are of medical importance. [16] Pine processionary caterpillars ( Thaumetopoea pityocampa ) are the important defoliator of pine forests in the Mediterranean and central European countries, with significant economic and occupational consequences for workers in these forests. [17] The Anaphe venata caterpillar in Nigeria is an essential protein resource but can cause thiamine deficiency syndrome similar to dry beriberi. [18] [19] Additionally, dendrolimus caterpillars in China and Preolis semirufa caterpillars in Brazil are linked to significant joint disease. [20] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Immediate Hypersensitivity Reactions (Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Multiple causes of IHR depend on the type of antigen or allergen that triggers this inappropriate immune reactivity. In type I hypersensitivity reactions, the allergens are proteins with a molecular weight ranging from 10 to 40 kDa. These include cats, dust mites, German cockroaches, grass, rats, fungi, plants, and drugs. They stimulate IgE production. Bee and wasp venoms, tree nuts (e.g., almond, hazelnut, walnut, and cashew), eggs, milk, latex, antibiotics (e.g., cephalosporins), heterologous antisera, hormones (e.g., insulin), and others including shellfish and anesthetics can trigger anaphylaxis. [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Pathergy Test -- Interfering Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Results of the pathergy test have been known to vary widely between populations and even between different investigators studying the same population. The positivity rate for pathergy tests in BD is highest in countries along the Silk Route, which includes the Middle East, Far East, and Mediterranean Basin. [6] By contrast, BD patients from the US, UK, and other Northern European nations have significantly lower reported rates for positive pathergy tests. Numerous studies have also demonstrated a decline in the prevalence of positive pathergy tests over time since the 1980s. [12] Genetic, environmental, and procedure-related variations have all been proposed as possible factors that can influence and interfere with pathergy testing. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2731
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon, also known as adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) or corticotropin, is a peptide hormone that belongs to the broader class of amino acids, peptides, and proteins. Numerous oral liquid preparations list ACTH as an active ingredient, often under the designation “corticotropin.” These formulations combine ACTH with a wide array of additional substances, including epinephrine, various mineral salts (iodine, sodium chloride, potassium carbonate, calcium carbonate, phosphorus, arsenic trioxide, silver nitrate), vitamins (niacinamide, thiamine hydrochloride), amino acids (aspartic acid, glutamic acid), nucleic acids (Saccharomyces cerevisiae RNA, herring sperm DNA), plant extracts (Mucuna pruriens seed, Anacardium occidentale fruit, Clematis recta flowering top, Datura stramonium, Atropa belladonna, Berberis vulgaris root bark, Phytolacca americana root, Nutmeg, Anemone pulsatilla, Lyco­podium clavatum spore), animal gland extracts (suspensions of sheep thyroid, adrenal gland, pituitary gland, hypothalamus, parathyroid gland, posterior pituitary, placenta), and other bioactive compounds (insulin, adenosine triphosphate, ubidecarenone, melatonin, testosterone, aldosterone, corticorelin, prasterone). Specific product complexes—such as OHM Pituitarum Complex, FCS HP, MBM 11 Thyroid Adrenal, and HP Axis—use these multi‑component mixtures to deliver ACTH alongside agents that may support or modulate endocrine function, cardiovascular response, metabolic processes, and immune activity. The inclusion of ACTH in these diverse formulations reflects its therapeutic role in stimulating adrenal cortisol production and managing conditions related to adrenal insufficiency or other disorders of the hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1321
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon is a peptide‑derived hormone that may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, reflecting its potential therapeutic role in demyelinating disease. This hormone is classified as a member of the peptide family, indicating its biochemical nature as a peptide molecule. In clinical formulations, adrenokortikotropní hormon is presented as an oral liquid whose active ingredient profile comprises a complex mixture that includes adenosine cyclic phosphate, corticotropin, onion extract, Ambrosia artemisiifolia, arsenic trioxide, Arisaema triphyllum root, Arundo pliniana root, cortisone acetate, Euphrasia stricta, Galphimia glauca flowering top, histamine dihydrochloride, potassium chloride, Sus scrofa nasal mucosa, naphthalene, sodium chloride, Saccharomyces cerevisiae RNA, Schoenocaulon officinale seed, sulfur, Wyethia helenioides root, among other components. This extensive composition defines the pharmacologically active constituents of the preparation and underscores the need for careful consideration of potential allergenic and safety issues when employing adrenokortikotropní hormon in patient care.

These extensive ingredient profiles illustrate that adrenokortikotropní hormon is employed in multi‑component oral preparations aimed at influencing the hypothalamic‑pituitary‑adrenal axis, supporting endocrine function, modulating neuro‑chemical activity, and addressing a broad range of physiological and therapeutic targets.

retrieved_docs.content: The oral liquid preparation listed as “ADENOSINE CYCLIC PHOSPHATE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / CORTICOTROPIN 30 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ONION 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / AMBROSIA ARTEMISIIFOLIA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ARSENIC TRIOXIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ARISAEMA TRIPHYLLUM ROOT 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ARUNDO PLINIANA ROOT 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / CORTISONE ACETATE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / EUPHRASIA STRICTA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / GALPHIMIA GLAUCA FLOWERING TOP 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / HISTAMINE DIHYDROCHLORIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / POTASSIUM CHLORIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SUS SCROFA NASAL MUCOSA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / NAPHTHALENE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SODIUM CHLORIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SACCHAROMYCES CEREVISIAE RNA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SCHOENOCAULON OFFICINALE SEED 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SULFUR 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / WYETHIA HELENIOIDES ROOT 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL ORAL LIQUID [Allergies]” contains corticotropin (30 [hp_X] in 118 mL) as an active ingredient. Corticotropin is the adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH), a peptide hormone that belongs to the broader biochemical class of amino acids, peptides, or proteins. Recognizing ACTH as a protein‑derived hormone clarifies its mechanism of action and informs its therapeutic use within this complex multi‑ingredient formulation.

The coexistence of neurotransmitters (norepinephrine, serotonin), hormone precursors (levodopa), mineral and botanical components, and peptide hormones (ACTH) in these multi‑component liquids suggests a therapeutic strategy aimed at modulating neuro‑endocrine function, adrenal activity, and possibly immune or allergic responses. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2526
retrieved_docs.content: Chaste tree preparation is described as a multi‑component medicinal formulation that appears in several dosage forms, each containing a distinct set of active ingredients or moieties. In the sublingual solution and drops marketed as “R18 OVAGEN,” the preparation lists adenosine triphosphate, ammonium chloride, platinum, progesterone, Apis mellifera, chaste tree, ammonium carbonate, and Lycopodium clavatum spore among its active ingredients. The oral pellet included in the “Joette Calabrese Survivalist Kit” is an even broader chaste tree preparation, comprising chaste tree together with a long list of botanical extracts (e.g., Artemis abrotanum, Aconitum napellus, Aethusa cynapium, Arnica montana, Atropa belladonna, Bapitisia tinctoria, Borrelia burgdorferi, Crotalus horridus venom, Daphne mezereum bark, Na ja na ja venom, Vipera berus venom, and many others), minerals (e.g., gold, barium carbonate, copper, lead, mercury compounds), acids (hydrofluoric, hydrochloric, phosphoric, nitric, picric, sulfuric), and synthetic chemicals (e.g., quinine sulfate, phenol, cadmium sulfate, bromine). In the oral spray product “OHM‑DAM,” the chaste tree preparation is defined by its active moiety, chaste tree fruit, and is combined with Avena sativa flowering top, Turnera diffusa leafy twig, Gelsemium sempervirens root, Strychnos ignatii seed, Lycopodium clavatum spore, Bos taurus testicle, phosphoric acid, saw palmetto, selenium, Tribulus terrestris whole, and yohimbine. Across all formulations, chaste tree (whether as fruit, extract, or other part) is consistently identified as a key active component, but each product also incorporates a unique constellation of additional botanical, mineral, and chemical substances that together constitute the therapeutic profile of the respective chaste tree preparation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 1958
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon is classified as an amino acid, peptide, or protein, indicating that it is a peptide hormone. It serves as a parent category for the entity “Hypothalamic and pituitary hormone, NOS,” meaning that this hypothalamic‑pituitary hormone is a specific subtype within the broader group of adrenokortikotropní hormon. In addition, adrenokortikotropní hormon is listed as an active ingredient in a complex oral liquid formulation described as “ADENOSINE CYCLIC PHOSPHATE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / CORTICOTROPIN 30 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ONION 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / AMBROSIA ARTEMISIIFOLIA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ARSENIC TRIOXIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ARISAEMA TRIPHYLLUM ROOT 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ARUNDO PLINIANA ROOT 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / CORTISONE ACETATE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / EUPHRASIA STRICTA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / GALPHIMIA GLAUCA FLOWERING TOP 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / HISTAMINE DIHYDROCHLORIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / POTASSIUM CHLORIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SUS SCROFA NASAL MUCOSA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / NAPHTHALENE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SODIUM CHLORIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SACCHAROMYCES CEREVISIAE RNA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SCHOENOCAULON OFFICINALE SEED 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SULFUR 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / WYETHIA HELENIOIDES ROOT 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL ORAL LIQUID [Allergies]”. This indicates that adrenokortikotropní hormon is utilized therapeutically within a multi‑component oral solution intended for allergy management. Together, these relationships highlight that adrenokortikotropní hormon is a peptide‑based hormone that underlies certain hypothalamic‑pituitary hormones and is employed as an active pharmaceutical ingredient in a complex oral allergy preparation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 1641
retrieved_docs.content: The oral liquid formulation described as “ADENOSINE CYCLIC PHOSPHATE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / CORTICOTROPIN 30 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ONION 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / AMBROSIA ARTEMISIIFOLIA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ARSENIC TRIOXIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ARISAEMA TRIPHYLLUM ROOT 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / ARUNDO PLINIANA ROOT 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / CORTISONE ACETATE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / EUPHRASIA STRICTA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / GALPHIMIA GLAUCA FLOWERING TOP 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / HISTAMINE DIHYDROCHLORIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / POTASSIUM CHLORIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SUS SCROFA NASAL MUCOSA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / NAPHTHALENE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SODIUM CHLORIDE 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SACCHAROMYCES CEREVISIAE RNA 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SCHOENOCAULON OFFICINALE SEED 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / SULFUR 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL / WYETHIA HELENIOIDES ROOT 12 [hp_X] in 118 mL ORAL LIQUID [Allergies]” has the active ingredient adrenokortikotropní hormon (adrenocorticotropic hormone, ACTH). ACTH is classified as an amino acid, peptide, or protein, reflecting its nature as a peptide hormone. Similarly, erythropoietin is also an amino acid, peptide, or protein, indicating that both ACTH and erythropoietin belong to the same broad biochemical class of peptide‑based hormones. This relationship underscores the common molecular foundation of these therapeutic agents, which are derived from proteinaceous precursors and function through hormone‑mediated pathways.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 367
retrieved_docs.content: The oral liquid preparation indicated for allergies is a complex multi‑component formulation that incorporates a wide array of active ingredients. Among these, adrenokortikotropní hormon (adrenocorticotropic hormone, ACTH) is present; ACTH is classified as a peptide hormone, indicating that it exerts its effects through peptide‑based signaling pathways. The formulation also contains potassium chloride, an electrolyte that contributes to ionic balance and fluid homeostasis. In addition to ACTH and potassium chloride, the product includes adenosine cyclic phosphate, corticotropin, onion, Ambrosia artemisiifolia, arsenic trioxide, Arisaema triphyllum root, Arundo pliniana root, cortisone acetate, Euphrasia stricta, Galphimia glauca flowering top, histamine dihydrochloride, sodium chloride, Saccharomyces cerevisiae RNA, Schoenocaulon officinale seed, sulfur, Wyethia helenioides root, naphtalene, and other salts such as sodium chloride. The inclusion of corticotropin and cortisone acetate adds glucocorticoid‑like activity, while histamine dihydrochloride provides antihistaminic effects. The numerous botanical extracts (onion, Ambrosia artemisiifolia, Arisaema triphyllum root, Arundo pliniana root, Euphrasia stricta, Galphimia glauca, Schoenocaulon officinale seed, Wyethia helenioides root) suggest additional anti‑inflammatory or immunomodulatory actions. Mineral components (sodium chloride, potassium chloride, sulfur) and compounds such as arsenic trioxide and naphtalene may serve as preservatives, stabilizers, or have specific pharmacologic roles. Together, these ingredients create a multi‑modal therapeutic approach that combines peptide hormone activity, electrolyte support, antihistamine action, glucocorticoid‑like effects, and botanical‑derived mechanisms to manage allergic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1441
retrieved_docs.content: Adrenokortikotropní hormon, which functions as the parent category for the nonspecific hypothalamic and pituitary hormone (NOS), is also the pharmacologically active component of a complex oral liquid formulation indicated for allergies. This formulation comprises a broad spectrum of ingredients, each present at 12 hp_X in 118 mL, including adenosine cyclic phosphate, corticotropin, onion extract, Ambrosia artemisiifolia, arsenic trioxide, Arisaema triphyllum root, Arundo pliniana root, cortisone acetate, Euphrasia stricta, Galphimia glauca flowering top, histamine dihydrochloride, potassium chloride, Sus scrofa nasal mucosa, naphthalene, sodium chloride, Saccharomyces cerevisiae RNA, Schoenocaulon officinale seed, sulfur, and Wyethia helenioides root. The presence of adrenokortikotropní hormon as the active ingredient links this hormonal classification to the therapeutic use of the multi‑ingredient preparation, underscoring its dual role as a parent hormone entity and as a key active agent in a composite allergy medication.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 733
retrieved_docs.content: The chaste tree preparation is classified as a member of the “other gynecologicals in ATC” group, which is a subclass of the broader “GENITO‑URINARY SYSTEM AND SEX HORMONES” category; this category in turn belongs to the “GYNECOLOGICAL ANTI‑INFECTIVES AND ANTISEPTICS” class. As a gynecological product, the preparation contains a complex mixture of active ingredients and active moieties. In one formulation, the sublingual solution/drops (R18 OVAGEN) lists adenosine‑triphosphate, ammonium chloride, platinum, progesterone, apis mellifera, chaste tree, ammonium carbonate and lycopodium clavatum spore among its active ingredients. A second oral pellet formulation (Joette Calabrese Survivalist Kit) includes an extensive array of substances such as artemisia abrotanum, aconitum napellus, chaste tree, various mineral salts (e.g., ammonium carbonate, antimony trisulfide, barium carbonate), metals (gold, copper, cadmium, cobalt, lead, mercury compounds), plant extracts (e.g., arnica montana, milk thistle, hyoscyamus niger, veratrum album), animal venoms (e.g., Bothrops atrox, Naja naja, Vipera berus), and numerous acids and organic compounds (e.g., hydrofluoric acid, nitric acid, phenol, quinine sulfate). A third formulation described as an oral spray (OHM‑DAM) identifies active moieties such as chaste tree fruit, avena sativa flowering top, turnera diffusa leafy twig, gelsemium sempervirens root, strychnos ignatii seed, lycopodium clavatum spore, bos taurus testicle, phosphoric acid, saw palmetto, selenium, tribulus terrestris whole, and yohimbine. Together, these details illustrate that the chaste tree preparation is a multi‑component gynecological agent, positioned within the ATC hierarchy of sex‑hormone and genitourinary therapies, and formulated with a wide spectrum of botanical, mineral, metallic, and synthetic constituents intended for therapeutic use in women’s health.
